subject: ap-chemistry
title: Alloys, Electrochemistry & Electrolysis
stimulus:
Sterling silver is an alloy that is commonly used to make jewelry and consists of 92.5% silver and 7.5% other metals, such as copper, by mass. Over time, the alloy can form a tarnish of $Ag_2S$(s) when it reacts with hydrogen sulfide, as represented by the following equation.

2 Ag(s) + $H_2S$(g) → $Ag_2S$(s) + $H_2$(g)

parts.prompt:
What are the oxidation numbers of silver in Ag(s) and $Ag_2S$(s)?

Ag(s) __________ $Ag_2S$(s) __________
parts.scoring_notes: Ag in Ag(s) = 0 (elemental). In Ag₂S: S is −2 (per convention), so each Ag = +1.

scoring_points.description: For both correct oxidation numbers (Ag(s) = 0, Ag in Ag₂S = +1): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: Ag(s) = 0 (pure element). In Ag₂S: S has oxidation number −2; since 2(Ag) + (−2) = 0, each Ag = +1.

wrong_examples: Ag in Ag₂S = +2 — there are 2 Ag atoms each with +1, giving a total of +2, but each individual Ag is +1.
common_traps: Distribute the charge per atom: Ag₂S is neutral; S = −2; so each of 2 Ag atoms = +1 (not +2 total per atom).

parts.prompt:
The following table contains the atomic radii for silver and copper.

| Element | Silver (Ag) | Copper (Cu) |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| Atomic radius (pm) | 165 | 145 |

(i) Explain why sterling silver is better classified as a substitutional alloy than as an interstitial alloy.

(ii) Using principles of atomic structure and Coulomb's law, explain why silver has a larger atomic radius than copper does.

The $Ag_2S$ tarnish on sterling silver can be removed until only sterling silver remains. A student weighs a tarnished sterling silver sample both before and after removing the $Ag_2S$(s) (molar mass 247.80 g/mol) and records the data in the following table.

| | Before Tarnish Removal | After Tarnish Removal |
|-|------------------------|----------------------|
| Mass | 409.21 g | 398.94 g |
parts.scoring_notes: Part (b)(i): substitutional because Ag and Cu have similar atomic radii (~10% difference), so Cu can substitute for Ag in the lattice. Part (b)(ii): Ag (n=5 valence shell) vs Cu (n=4 valence shell); more electron shells → greater distance from nucleus → larger radius (Coulombic reasoning: larger r reduces nuclear attraction).

scoring_points.description: For a valid explanation citing similar atomic radii making substitution possible: 1 point

required_elements: Silver and copper have similar atomic radii (165 pm vs. 145 pm, within ~15%).
Cu atoms can replace (substitute for) Ag atoms in the silver lattice rather than fitting into interstitial gaps.
alternatives.correct_example: Substitutional alloys form when the solute and solvent atoms have similar sizes. The atomic radii of Ag (165 pm) and Cu (145 pm) differ by about 12%, which is within the range where one atom can substitute for the other in the crystal lattice. In an interstitial alloy, the solute would be much smaller and fit into gaps between lattice atoms — Cu is not small enough to be interstitial.

wrong_examples: Saying they are interstitial because Cu is smaller — Cu is not small enough to occupy interstitial holes in the Ag lattice.
common_traps: Interstitial alloys require a much smaller solute atom (e.g., carbon in steel); substitutional alloys require similar sizes.

scoring_points.description: For a Coulomb's law explanation citing electron shells and nuclear distance: 1 point

required_elements: Ag has more occupied electron shells than Cu (Ag: n=5; Cu: n=4).
Additional electron shells mean the valence electrons are farther from the nucleus, resulting in a larger radius. Per Coulomb's law, greater distance reduces nuclear attraction.
alternatives.correct_example: Ag has electrons in the n = 5 shell while Cu has electrons in the n = 4 shell. The additional occupied shell in Ag places the outermost electrons farther from the nucleus. By Coulomb's law ($F \propto 1/r^2$), the nuclear attraction decreases with increasing distance, and electrons are held less tightly, resulting in a larger atomic radius for Ag.

wrong_examples: Saying Cu has a larger nucleus — nuclear charge (proton number) in Cu (Z=29) and Ag (Z=47) differs, but the shell argument is primary.
common_traps: Must reference Coulomb's law and distance (r) explicitly for full credit.

parts.prompt:
Assuming that only $Ag_2S$(s) is removed, calculate the number of moles of silver atoms removed.

Rhodium plating is a process used to protect sterling silver from tarnishing. This involves electroplating (depositing) solid rhodium, Rh(s), onto the surface of the metal from an acidified solution of $Rh_2(SO_4)_3$(aq). Oxygen gas is produced during this process.
parts.scoring_notes: Mass of Ag₂S removed = 409.21 − 398.94 = 10.27 g. Moles Ag₂S = 10.27/247.80. Moles Ag = 2 × moles Ag₂S.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated mass of Ag₂S removed (may be implicit): 1 point
wrong_examples: Using 398.94 as the mass of Ag₂S — that is the mass of the cleaned sample, not the tarnish.
common_traps: The mass change (before − after) equals the mass of Ag₂S removed, not the mass of the sample remaining.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated moles of Ag atoms: 1 point
wrong_examples: Forgetting to multiply by 2 — each formula unit of Ag₂S contains 2 Ag atoms.
Using molar mass of Ag (107.9 g/mol) instead of Ag₂S (247.80 g/mol) in the denominator.
common_traps: Ag₂S contains 2 Ag atoms per formula unit; multiply moles of Ag₂S by 2 to get moles of Ag.

parts.prompt:
A table of half-reactions related to the overall reaction is provided.

| Half-Reaction | E° (V) |
|--------------|--------|
| $Rh^{3+}$(aq) + 3$e^-$ → Rh(s) | +0.80 |
| $O_2$(g) + 4$H^+$(aq) + 4$e^-$ → 2$H_2O$(l) | +1.23 |

(i) Write the balanced net ionic equation for plating Rh(s) from the acidified $Rh_2(SO_4)_3$(aq) solution.

(ii) Calculate the value of $E°_{cell}$ for the reaction in part (d)(i).

(iii) Based on your answer to part (d)(ii), explain why this process requires the use of an external power source.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (d)(i): balance electrons (LCM of 3 and 4 = 12); reverse O₂ half-reaction (oxidation at anode). Part (d)(ii): E°_cell = E°_cathode − E°_anode = 0.80 − 1.23 = −0.43 V. Part (d)(iii): negative E°_cell → reaction not spontaneous → needs external voltage.

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced net ionic equation: 1 point
wrong_examples: Not balancing electrons (LCM = 12): Rh half-reaction × 4 (12 e⁻); O₂ half-reaction × 3 (12 e⁻).
common_traps: Electrolysis: Rh³⁺ is reduced at cathode; water is oxidized at anode to produce O₂ in acid.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated E°_cell (−0.43 V): 1 point
wrong_examples: Adding 0.80 + 1.23 = 2.03 V — must subtract (E°_cathode − E°_anode) not add both reduction potentials.
common_traps: For electrolytic cells, E°_cell is negative, indicating a non-spontaneous reaction.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation connecting negative E°_cell to thermodynamic non-spontaneity: 1 point

required_elements: $E°_{\text{cell}} < 0$ (−0.43 V), which means $\Delta G° > 0$ — the reaction is not spontaneous.
An external power source must supply the required electrical energy to drive the non-spontaneous reaction.
alternatives.correct_example: Since $E°_{\text{cell}} = -0.43\text{ V} < 0$, $\Delta G° = -nFE°_{\text{cell}} > 0$. The positive $\Delta G°$ indicates the reaction is thermodynamically non-spontaneous and will not proceed on its own. An external power source (voltage source) must be used to supply the electrical energy needed to drive the plating reaction forward.

wrong_examples: Saying the reaction needs activation energy — this is a thermodynamic argument about spontaneity, not kinetics.
common_traps: Negative E°_cell ↔ positive ΔG° ↔ non-spontaneous; external voltage supplies the energy deficit.

parts.prompt:
Calculate the length of time, in seconds, required to plate 2.8 g of Rh(s )onto a piece of sterling silver if
2.0 C / s of current is applied.
parts.scoring_notes: Rh molar mass = 102.9 g/mol; Rh³⁺ requires 3 e⁻ per Rh. Moles electrons = (2.8/102.9)×3. Time = charge/current = (moles e⁻ × 96485 C/mol) / 2.0 A.

scoring_points.description: For the correct moles of electrons (may be implicit): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$n(\text{Rh}) = 2.8/102.9 = 0.0272\text{ mol}$ 
$n(e^-) = 3 \times 0.0272 = 0.0816\text{ mol}$ (3 electrons per Rh³⁺ → Rh)

wrong_examples: Using 1 or 2 electrons per Rh — Rh³⁺ requires 3 electrons to reduce to Rh(s).
common_traps: Rh³⁺ + 3e⁻ → Rh(s): 3 faradays per mole of Rh deposited.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated time in seconds: 1 point
wrong_examples: Dividing moles by current instead of converting moles to coulombs first.
Using Faraday's constant as 9.65×10⁴ C/mol — accept this value.
common_traps: Faraday's constant: 1 mol electrons = 96,485 C. Time (s) = charge (C) ÷ current (A = C/s).
